title: India-Nepal Bilateral Expansion: EAM Jaishankar Charts Course Beyond Energy into AI, Startups
lede: External Affairs Minister Dr. S. Jaishankar met Nepal's Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal in Delhi on June 6, 2026, signaling a strategic pivot to expand bilateral cooperation into emerging technology sectors while maintaining traditional pillars of energy and security.

sections.context: The bilateral meeting between India's EAM and Nepal's Foreign Minister reflects a deliberate recalibration of the India-Nepal relationship architecture. Jaishankar's opening remarks anchored the discussion in the established foundation of people-to-people ties, cultural exchange, and cross-border connectivity that have historically defined the relationship. However, the emphasis on newer domains—startups, artificial intelligence, information technology, and renewable energy—signals New Delhi's intent to deepen engagement beyond the conventional energy cooperation framework that has dominated bilateral ties for decades. The timing is significant: the meeting occurs against the backdrop of regional instability in West Asia and the Middle East, which Jaishankar explicitly referenced when noting that India has maintained uninterrupted fuel supplies to Nepal during the ongoing crisis. This demonstrates how India leverages its strategic position to reinforce bilateral trust and demonstrate reliability during periods of international volatility.
sections.threads: This bilateral engagement fits within a broader pattern of India's strategic recalibration toward Nepal, part of a longer-term effort to institutionalize and modernize the relationship framework. [[india-np-india-nepal-bilateral-pivot-eam-jaishankar-signals-expa-2026|Previous bilateral meetings have already signaled movement toward AI and startup ecosystems]], indicating this is not an isolated conversation but part of an ongoing strategic pivot. The security dimension remains central to Indian interests—Jaishankar explicitly referenced close collaboration to maintain security along the long and open border—a persistent concern that has occasionally generated friction, including past [[india-np-nepals-cartographic-claim-how-a-map-became-a-strategic-crisi-2020|cartographic disputes]]. The expansion into technology sectors also reflects India's broader regional strategy of positioning itself as a preferred partner in emerging economic domains, competing with other regional and global powers for influence in Nepal's development trajectory.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor whether Nepal follows through with concrete institutional mechanisms to operationalize cooperation in AI and startups, or whether these remain rhetorical commitments. The next decision points will likely emerge around: (1) whether bilateral working groups or dedicated task forces are established for technology cooperation; (2) the pace at which renewable energy projects expand beyond traditional hydropower frameworks; and (3) whether Nepal's political establishment prioritizes [[india-np-india-nepal-bilateral-deepening-eam-jaishankar-charts-e-2026|institutional deepening of this bilateral agenda]] or permits it to stall amid domestic political pressures. Given the emphasis on mutual benefit and shared development, the depth of Indian investment commitments in Nepal's startup and technology sectors will serve as a tangible metric of strategic intent. The maintenance of fuel supplies during the West Asia crisis also sets a precedent that India may leverage in future bilateral negotiations around security, border management, and development cooperation priorities.
